title: Rule Reach Through Interpretation Drift
concept: A rule's practical reach changes when interpreters add requirements not in the original text — each new standard creates a gap between what the rule says and what it covers, allowing the rule to remain unchanged while what it does shifts completely.

questions.question: Why does adding proof requirements not written in a rule shrink what the rule covers more effectively than removing the rule entirely?
options: Added requirements sound technical rather than political, so they avoid immediate pushback
Removing a rule requires formal approval; adding interpretation requirements happens through individual decisions that look like normal application
People trust courts more than legislatures to decide what rules should mean
Interpretation requirements can be removed more easily if they prove too strict
questions.explanation: Striking down a rule triggers visibility and opposition — the change is obvious, the decision is challenged, the institution's legitimacy is questioned. Adding proof requirements case by case looks like normal legal work: clarifying standards, applying general language to specific facts. Each decision appears narrow and technical. By the time the cumulative effect is clear, the pattern is embedded across multiple settled cases. The rule stays on the books, opposition is diffused, and what the rule does has transformed.

questions.question: What makes requiring proof of harm 'compared to past industry standards' harder to meet than requiring proof of harm right now?
options: Historical data is harder to find and easier to dispute than current measurements
Past standards were stricter, so modern harm looks minor by comparison
It adds a second test — harm must exist now AND exceed what was tolerated before, turning a single-gate check into a double-gate filter
Comparing across time periods makes the analysis more objective
questions.explanation: The harm might be measurable and real today — injury rates up, pollution levels high, access restricted. But if similar conditions existed under past rules, the new test says that's not enough. You must prove current harm AND prove it's worse than the historical baseline. The requirement doesn't ask 'is there harm?' — it asks 'is there more harm than there used to be?' Most rules don't include historical records, so proving 'more than before' requires data that often doesn't exist.

questions.question: How does a balancing requirement convert a prohibition into a judgment call?
options: It gives decision-makers authority to weigh competing priorities rather than follow a fixed rule
Balancing makes the rule more precise by forcing consideration of context
It adds a second interest that can override the first — even when the original rule applies, the interpreter decides whether preventing harm matters more than the competing concern
Balancing is required when multiple rules conflict
questions.explanation: A prohibition says 'X is not allowed.' A balancing test says 'X is not allowed unless Y is more important, and we decide what's more important.' The rule no longer has a clear answer — it has a framework for making trade-offs. This shifts every case from 'Did X happen?' (factual, yes-or-no) to 'Does stopping X matter more than allowing Y?' (judgment, varies by decision-maker). The prohibition becomes conditional on winning a weighing contest.

questions.question: Why does spreading new interpretation requirements across multiple decisions make the pattern harder to reverse than announcing all of them at once?
options: Each decision creates precedent, so later decisions are constrained by earlier ones — reversing the pattern means overturning a chain of settled cases
Multiple decisions give the new interpretation more time to become accepted practice
Gradual changes are less disruptive to the people relying on the rule
Courts are required to change interpretation incrementally rather than all at once
questions.explanation: Decision one adds requirement A. Decision two adds requirement B, citing decision one as precedent. Decision three adds requirement C, now citing both prior decisions. Each builds on the last. By the time the full pattern is visible, undoing it means overturning not one decision but an entire sequence where each relied on the previous one. Announcing A, B, and C together makes the transformation obvious and easy to challenge as a single departure. Spacing them out bakes each requirement into the foundation before the next arrives.

questions.question: When does adding interpretation requirements fail to change what a rule actually blocks?
options: When the people trying to use the rule can meet all the new proof standards in most cases
When the requirements contradict each other and cancel out
When public attention forces courts to reverse the interpretation
When the original rule language is too clear to reinterpret
questions.explanation: Requirements shrink a rule's reach only if they actually filter cases. If everyone affected by the rule can produce the required evidence, meet the elevated standards, and win the balancing test, the added requirements don't block claims — they just add steps. The strategy depends on requirements that sound reasonable but turn out to be hard to satisfy in practice. If 90% of cases still succeed after jumping through the new hoops, the rule's practical reach hasn't narrowed. The procedure got longer; the outcome stayed the same.
